coredata.dc:title: Quantum dot- β-Cyclodextrin nanofiller decorated thin film nanocomposite membrane for removal of cationic and anionic dyes from aqueous solution
coredata.dc:creator: Goswami, Rajiv
Gogoi, Monti
Borah, Alimpia
Sarmah, Hrishikesh
Borah, Akhil Ranjan
Feng, Xianshe
Hazarika, Swapnali
coredata.dc:description: Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) are finding tremendous attention as nanofillers in separation processes owing to their excellent chemical and mechanical robustness. GOQDs-functionalized thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes were fabricated via low pressure mediated interfacial polymerization (IP) over a polysulfone support, where β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD) was taken as an aqueous monomer with a fractional amount of GOQDs and tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) as the organic monomer. The resultant TFN membranes showed improved hydrophilicity over its pristine polymeric analogue. The best performing membrane was obtained for MPG2, which had a mass loading of 3 % of β-CD, 0.3 % of TEPA and 0.4 % of GOQD during the interfacial polymerization. The membrane showed pure water flux that was two fold higher than that of the pristine polymeric membrane, with a dye rejection of 99.6 % for congo red and 96 % for methyl blue. This excellent rejection for the anionic dye can be attributed to the Donnan exclusion imparted by the negative charges on the membrane surface. The salt rejection properties of the membranes were also analyzed and the MPG2 membrane exhibited a high rejection rate of 96 % and 99.1 % for the salt containing Cl− and NO3 − ions, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Nanofiltration membrane
Dyes
Antifouling
Rejection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Studies on the rejection of dye
Studies on antifouling behaviour of the membranes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: CSIR-North East Institute of Science and Technology
Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors express sincere gratitude to CSIR New Delhi for the research grant, and extend heartfelt appreciation to the Director, CSIR-NEIST for steadfast support in advancing the progress of this research group. Authors acknowledge Analytical Chemistry Group and Sophisticated Analytical Instrument Facility, CSIR NEIST, for some analytical work.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of fabrication of PA–GOQD thin film membrane.

Schematic diagram of the experimental set-up (1) membrane, (2) membrane cell, (3) pressure gauze, (4) valve, (5) nitrogen cylinder, (6) peristaltic pump, (7) feed tank, (8) gas outlet (9) magnetic stirrer (10) sample vials.
FTIR spectra of (a) PA–GOQD thin-film membrane, MPG2 (b) PSf membrane.
XPS spectra of (A1) Survey scan; (A2, B2) C1s; (A3, B3) O1s; (A4, B4) S2p high-resolution scan of PSf and MPG2 membrane; (B1) N1s of MPG2 membrane.
FESEM images of MPG1 (A1, A2), MPG2 (B1, B2) and MPG3 (C1, C2) membranes and TEM images of MPG1 (A3), MPG2 (B3) and MPG3 (C3) membranes.
(A) Contact angle (B) Zeta Potential of PSf, MPG0, MPG1, MPG2 and MPG3 (C) AFM images of (C.1) MPG0, (C.2) MPG1, (C.3) MPG2 and (C.4) MPG3 membranes.
(A) Water flux of MPG0, MPG1, MPG2 and MPG3 membranes (B) % Rejection of MPG2 membrane for different dyes.
(A) Salt rejection (B) dynamic fouling studies of TFN membranes.
Properties of the dyes employed in the permeation study from a physical perspective.

head.ce:title: Quantum dot- β-Cyclodextrin nanofiller decorated thin film nanocomposite membrane for removal of cationic and anionic dyes from aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of Waterloo, Waterloo, ON, N2L 3G1, Canada

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Chemical Engineering Group and Centre for Petroleum Research, CSIR-North East Institute of Science and Technology, Jorhat-785006, Assam, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chemical Engineering Group and Centre for Petroleum Research
CSIR-North East Institute of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) are finding tremendous attention as nanofillers in separation processes owing to their excellent chemical and mechanical robustness. GOQDs-functionalized thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes were fabricated via low pressure mediated interfacial polymerization (IP) over a polysulfone support, where β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD) was taken as an aqueous monomer with a fractional amount of GOQDs and tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA) as the organic monomer. The resultant TFN membranes showed improved hydrophilicity over its pristine polymeric analogue. The best performing membrane was obtained for MPG2, which had a mass loading of 3 % of β-CD, 0.3 % of TEPA and 0.4 % of GOQD during the interfacial polymerization. The membrane showed pure water flux that was two fold higher than that of the pristine polymeric membrane, with a dye rejection of 99.6 % for congo red and 96 % for methyl blue. This excellent rejection for the anionic dye can be attributed to the Donnan exclusion imparted by the negative charges on the membrane surface. The salt rejection properties of the membranes were also analyzed and the MPG2 membrane exhibited a high rejection rate of 96 % and 99.1 % for the salt containing Cl− and NO3 − ions, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the dye and pigment industry has witnessed significant growth in the production of pigments, dyes, and intermediates. Efficient removal of various inorganic compounds, a crucial step in synthesizing dyes and pigments from intermediates, is essential to enhance their characteristic parameters, such as applicability and stability [1,2]. The pollutants discharge from textile industries are mainly high salinity, colored organics and inorganics, recalcitrant organics, and toxic intermediates, posing substantial risks to human health and the ecosystem. The non-biodegradable nature and toxicity of dyestuffs contribute to irreversible environmental damage. Consequently, treating dyeing wastewater from textile industries stands as one of the foremost challenges in environmental remediation [2]. Addressing this issue requires the application of efficient separation techniques with enhanced selectivity to remove salted and concentrated dyestuffs from wastewater, a process crucial for resource recovery in the chemical process industry.

In contemporary wastewater treatment for dyeing processes, several conventional methods exist, but membrane processes stand out due to their high selectivity, low operating costs, and compact design [3]. Among membrane processes, nanofiltration (NF) has gained prominence over ultrafiltration (UF) for textile industry wastewater treatment. NF effectively separates small-sized organic molecules and dissolved ions, offering superior energy efficiency compared to reverse osmosis (RO) [4,5].

Jeong et al. used an interfacial polymerization (IP) process to fabricate TFN membranes where zeolite NaA nanoparticles were embedded in the thin film [6]. Yin et al. incorporated silica nanoparticles (e.g., MCM-41) into a TFN membrane for wastewater treatment, and the membrane performance was examined by comparing the surface active separation behaviour of the thinfilm composite (TFC) membrane to the raw polymeric membrane support [7]. Lee et al. described PA-TiO2 TFN membranes that demonstrated 95% rejection rate for MgSO4 and a water permeation flux of 9.1 L m−2h−1 under a transmembrane pressure of 0.6 MPa [8]. It is worth noting that the interphase of graphene oxide (GO) flakes may facilitate a faster transport rate for liquid water [9]. Moreover, the well defined interlayer space and well arranged laminar structure of GO also favor molecular cut-off and thus high selectivity [10]. GO flakes generally exhibit a negative charge with oxygen rich –COOH groups [11], and it can pose a charge effect for removing unwanted charged solute particles. Furthermore, the fouling resistance of the membrane to negatively charged foulants can be increased by increasing the charge density of GO [12]. Therefore, recent studies have emphasized on preparation of GO-based membranes and examining their performance in various applications. However, it is important to note that the smoothness or surface characteristics of GO flakes can vary depending on the fabrication process. While some studies have reported the presence of relatively smooth top surfaces in GO membranes, this characteristic is influenced by factors such as synthesis methods and parameters. Furthermore, GO flakes have been successfully utilized as nanofillers in TFN membranes for NF and RO applications, resulting in excellent membrane performance [13].

GOQDs consist of layered graphene sheets, each measuring several nanometers [14]. Their unique physical and chemical characteristics such as large surface area, high mobility and biocompatibility in various solvents, have positioned GOQDs at the forefront of membrane science and technology [15]. Notably, specific particle size, shape, and edge structure of GOQDs enable the creation of a well dispersed polymeric blend, ensuring that the multifunctional properties of the resulting membranes are preserved during synthesis. These attributes make the incorporation of GOQDs into polymeric matrices a promising avenue for enhancing membrane performance. In the context of NF membrane development, GOQDs have demonstrated their potential in addressing critical challenges. Zeng et al. [16] advanced this by introducing GOQD based polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membranes, which exhibited improved bactericidal and anti-biofouling capabilities. Additionally, Song et al. [17] undertook the integration of GOQDs into polymeric membranes designed for RO applications. These innovative membranes not only exhibited antifouling properties but also displayed remarkable resistance to chlorine, further underscoring the versatility and potential of GOQDs in membrane technology. The exceptional properties of GOQDs, such as their large surface area, mobility, and compatibility make them a pivotal component for NF membrane design, particularly in applications related to the dye industry.

A unique TFN membrane, incorporating GOQDs was fabricated through IP for efficient removal of colored dyestuffs and improved antifouling. The exceptional chemical and mechanical robustness of GOQDs significantly enhanced membrane hydrophilicity. Integration of GOQDs in the IP process improved rejection rates for dyes, such as congo red and methyl blue, showcasing remarkable selectivity. Negative charges on the GOQDdecorated membrane surface facilitated effective electrostatic repulsion, enhancing rejection of both anionic and cationic dyes, highlighting the pivotal role of GOQDs in achieving superior separation and antifouling properties.

The structure and performance of β-CD have aroused attention towards the membrane based dye separation. The advantage of β-CD can be attributed to its specific toroid structure accompanied by the inner cavity within its matrix, allowing the passage of water molecules throughout while rejecting organic dye molecules. This structure is noteworthy for improving membrane permeability without compromising its selectivity. In addition, abundant hydroxyl groups of β-CD can provide active sites on the membrane surface to ameliorate the support membrane. GOQDs possess carboxylic and hydroxyl groups at the edges of benzene ring. The two –NH2 groups present in tetraethylenepentamine(TEPA) with three alkyl substituted amines in the long hydrophobic alkyl chain can promote effective interactions between amine and carboxylic groups to form reactive –CONH- linkage. Thus, the TFN membranes so formed were expected to be effective for rejection of cationic and anionic dyes. The TFN membranes were characterized for morphology, charge properties and chemical composition, and their separation performance was examined in terms of water flux and rejection to organic dyes. In order to retain cationic and anionic dyes at low operating pressure while filtering water, the membrane's antifouling propensity was measured in terms of the flux recovery ratio (FRR), irreversible flux decline ratio (IFR), and relative fouled flux ratio (RFR).

Graphene oxide (GO), polysulfone (PSf) (M.W. 30,000 Da) were purchased from SIGMA-Aldrich, USA and used for membrane preparation. Magnesium nitrate (Mg(NO3)2), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), magnesium chloride (MgCl2) and sodium chloride (NaCl) were purchased from RANKEM, Gujarat, India, and used for salt rejection study. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) used for synthesis of GO was procured from RANKEM, Gujarat, India. Polyethylene glycol (PEG) was used as an additive and N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) used as solvent in fabricating microporous PSf substrate, and both chemicals were procured from TCI Chemicals, India. TCI Chemicals also provided tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA), β-Cyclodextrin (β-CD) and n-hexane, which were used for active layer formation via IP on the PSf substrate. Rhodamine B, congo red, methyl orange, methylene blue and crystal violet were used as model dyestuffs to study the membrane performance for treating textile wastewater, while bovine serum albumin (BSA) was used as a protein foulant to determine the antifouling behavior of the membranes. All these chemicals were procured from TCI Chemicals, India.

GOQDs were synthesized following the protocol reported in the literature [18]. 100 mg of GO and 18 mL of 3 wt% H2O2 were mixed to initiate the oxidation process and the solution was vigorously mixed under an ultrasonication bath for 30 min. Following that, the blend was moved to an autoclave lined with Teflon and heated at 180 °C for 2 h. The solution that resulted was light yellow in colour and was filtered through 0.2 mm filter paper to eliminate any impurities. After filtration, the resulting filtrate was subjected to centrifugation for 10 min, followed by 5–6 washes with deionized water until the solution reached a neutral pH. Subsequently, the collected product was dried using a vacuum dryer at 65 °C and obtained in powder form.

To prepare the support membrane, 11.5 g of PSf was dissolved in 50 ml of NMP and stirred at 45 °C for 10 h. The clear polymeric solution was then degassed and cast onto a clean glass plate. Subsequently, the glass plate was submerged in water, which acted as a non-solvent inducing phase inversion and resulting in a flat polymeric membrane.

In the first step, an aqueous monomer phase is prepared by dissolving β-CD at a concentration of 3 wt%. β-CD is a cyclic oligosaccharide that has a hydrophilic nature and can form intermolecular hydrogen bonds. To this aqueous solution, the desired amount of GOQDs (between 0 and 0.6 wt%) is added, allowing for the control of the GOQD concentration in the resulting membrane. Simultaneously, the organic monomer phase is prepared by dissolving TEPA at a concentration of 0.3 wt% in n-hexane. TEPA is an amine derivative with reactivity towards the formation of amide bonds. The PSf support is immersed in the aqueous monomer solution for 10 min. During this step, β-CD molecules solubilize in the aqueous phase. The support is then removed from the aqueous solution and dried at 40 °C. After drying, the PSf support is submerged in the organic monomer solution containing TEPA for 5 min. This step initiates the IP process. The prepared membrane was further oven dried to stabilize the polyamide (PA) layer.

In the presence of TEPA, a reaction takes place between the amine groups of TEPA in the organic phase and the hydroxyl groups of β-CD in the aqueous phase. This reaction leads to the formation of amide bonds (CONH) by removing water molecules. The pH of this reaction is maintained within an appropriate range of 6.9–7.2 without the need for an external catalytic agent. The effective interaction and bonding between the two phases, driven by intermolecular hydrogen bonding and amide bond formation play a crucial role in the successful development of the nanocomposite membrane. This approach eliminates the requirement for an additional catalytic agent in the IP process. Simultaneously, effective hydrogen bonding occurs between the GOQD/β-CD and TEPA, further enhancing the cross-linking between the aqueous and organic phases. The IP process ultimately results in the formation of a PA layer on the PSf support. This PA layer incorporates GOQDs, and its structure is stabilized by the amide bonds formed during the reaction as shown in Fig. 1 . The presence of β-CD and TEPA facilitates the effective interaction and bonding between the two phases, which leads to the successful development of the nanocomposite membrane. The hypothesized mechanism as shown in Fig. 2 , highlights the importance of intermolecular hydrogen bonding, amide bond formation, and effective interactions between the monomers in the IP process, which is crucial for the fabrication of the PA-GOQD TFN membrane. Table 1 illustrates the different types of membrane prepared by varying the concentration of GOQD.

GOQDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared analysis (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). The contact angles (CA) and interfacial surface energy of the membranes (PSf substrate, MPG1, MPG2 and MPG3 membranes) were determined using a contact angle meter (DM-501, Kyonea Interface Science). To calculate interfacial free energy, the modified Young-Dupre equation was used (GSL).
(1)
− Δ G _
( SL )
= γ _
( L )
[ 1 +
Cos
θ
Δ
]
Where γL is the liquid surface tension (for deionized water: γL = 73 mJ/m2), θ is the contact angle and Δ is the relative surface area of the membrane.

The surface charge properties were determined using a SurPASSTM electrokinetic analyzer (Anton Paar, Austria) at pH 7. Among the three prepared PA–GOQD thin film membranes, the membranes with good hydrophilicity and surface charges were further analyzed by FTIR on a PerkinElmer, 2000 and X-ray photoelectron spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific: ESCALAB Xi+), and these results were compared with pure PSf substrate membrane. The surface and cross-section images of the membranes were determined using a scanning electron microscope (LEO 1427 VP, UK) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEOL, Japan, JEM 2100). The thermal sensitivity of membranes was determined by a thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) technique, using a PERKIN Elmer PC series, DSC 7. The roughness of the membrane surface was measured by using an atomic force microscope (WITec Atomic Force Microscope alpha 300A).

For the permeation experiments, a two-compartment membrane set-up (as illustrated in Fig. 3 ) was used, with a membrane sample of 20 cm2 area inserted between the two compartments of the cell. The experiments were conducted for 8 h while maintaining the transmembrane pressure at 0.4 MPa. The permeate sample was collected from the permeate side at hourly intervals and its absorbance was measured using a Specord 200 UV visible spectrophotometer. The residues were collected by backwashing the membrane with deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies on the rejection of dye
ce:section.ce:para:
To assess the efficacy of the membranes in eliminating dyes, model pollutants comprising of both cationic and anionic dyes were employed. A 1000 ppm aqueous solution of dye was prepared and circulated through the membranes in dead-end mode under a pressure of 0.4 MPa. The concentration of dye in the permeate solution and the percentage of dye rejection were then calculated using a formula:
(2)
%
rejection =
[
1 −
D y e
c o n c e n t r a t i o n
i n
p e r m e a t e
D y e
c o n c e n t r a t i o n
i n
f e e d
]
x
100

The membrane flux can be calculated using the following equation,
(3)
Flux =
V
A Δ t

The assessment of dye rejection was also examined for different feed solutions containing NaCl, Na2SO4, MgCl2 and Mg(NO3)2, respectively, where the salt concentration was taken as 1000 ppm. Similarly, the salt rejection percentage was ascertained by computing the salt concentrations in both the feed and permeate solutions and applying a formula (2) similar to the one used for dye rejection calculations.

To evaluate the antifouling characteristics of the membranes, the FRR can be measured, which is determined by changes in permeate flux observed after conducting multiple cycles of permeation and cleaning experiments [19]. The antifouling behaviour of the membranes was evaluated using BSA as a representative protein model. A permeation test was conducted using an aqueous solution containing 1 g/L of BSA for three cycles, comprising the following steps: deionized water, BSA solution, and back-flushing of the membrane for 45 min with 1 × phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). The fouling resistance of the TFN membranes was evaluated using FRR, IFR and RFR:
(4)
FRR
( % )
=
J P
J W
× 100
(5)
IFR
( % )
= 100
–
FRR
(6)
RFR
( % )
=
J B
J W
× 100
The synthesized GOQDs were identified using FTIR, XPS and FESEM analysis. The detailed results are presented in the supplementary material, and the corresponding figures are shown in Figs. S1–S5.

FTIR analysis was done on MPG2 membrane and the base PSf substrate to confirm the formation of PA layer in MPG2, and the results were shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4(a) and (b) illustrate that the absorption peaks at 3301 cm−1 and 1752 cm−1 correspond to –OH and –CO stretching of unreacted amine and GOQDs, respectively in the PSf membrane. The presence of C–O–C, O=S=O, and alkyl groups in the PSf membrane is indicated by absorption peaks at a wavelength of 1232 cm−1, 1143 cm−1 and 2896 cm−1. The stretching and bending vibrations of the –NH bond is observed at 3312 cm−1 and 1598 cm−1, respectively, while the formation of a PA layer is confirmed by the –C=O stretching at 1664 cm−1 and –NH2 stretching at 3416 cm−1. These results confirm the successful formation of a PA layer on the PSf support [19,20].

Fig. 5(A1) presents the XPS survey scans of both the PSf substrate and the PA-GOQD TFN membrane MPG2. The scans confirm the presence of carbon (C1s), oxygen (O1s), and sulfur (S2p) in both membranes. In addition, the presence of nitrogen (N1s) is a distinctive feature of MPG2, indicating its structural difference from the PSf support. The high-resolution N1s scan of MPG2 is shown in Fig. 5 (B1), which shows a broad peak that resolves into two characteristic binding energy shifts at 398.7 and 400.1 eV, corresponding to the N–H and N–C=O bond of the PA-GOQD membrane [21,22].

The high resolution C1s scan of the PSf membrane displayed three clear peaks at 284.7, 286.5, and 285.9 eV, corresponding to the C–C, C–O, and C–S bonds respectively as shown in Fig. 5(A2). On the contrary, the C1s scan of the PA–GOQD represents characteristic binding energy shifts at 282.9, 283.4, 283.9, 284.5, 285.5 eV representing C–S, C=C, C–C, C–N and C=O respectively as shown in Fig. 5(B2) [23].

The high resolution O1s scan of MPG2 and PSf substrate is shown in Fig. 5(A3, B3) respectively. The scan of MPG2 revealed three distinctive binding energy shifts, while PSf substrate showed two different shifts within the range of 530–536 eV. The binding energy shift at 531.5 eV is obtained due to the presence of the carbonyl groups, which is an important aspect of the functionalization. The O1s high resolution scan revealed the binding energy shift at 532.5 eV for the O=S=O bond, while an intense and sharp peak of C–O was observed at 533.8 eV in both cases [22,24]. Moreover, the high-resolution scan of the sulfur atom has successfully resolved its broad peak, confirming C–S and O=S=O shifts at 167.6 and 168.1 eV, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 5(A4, B4).

The surface morphology and cross-sectional view of the TFN membranes MPG1, MPG2, and MPG3 were examined using FESEM. Fig. 6 (A1, B1, C1) displays the surface morphology of the membranes, indicating a significant variation with an increase in GOQD concentration. The cross-sections of the TFN membranes are shown in Fig. 6(A2, B2, C2), which confirmed the formation of a selective PA layer on the outer layer of the substrate. The GOQDs exhibited good compatibility at the interface of the interlayer of the composite membranes [25]. The surface micrographs reveal a sponge-like porous architecture, a characteristic that significantly contributes to the unimpeded water permeability of the TFN membranes.

HRTEM was employed to observe the internal structure of the nanocomposite membranes. The distribution of pores throughout the membrane in a largely homogeneous manner was shown in Fig. 6(A3, B3 and C3). The presence of cavities over the membrane was indicated by circular patches in the micrograph.

Membrane hydrophilicity is another critical parameter affecting water permeability and membrane antifouling properties. The degree of hydrophilicity of the membranes was assessed using a CA measuring instrument, whereby a lower CA indicates a more hydrophilic membrane surface with a stronger attraction to water molecules [25]. The PSf membrane exhibited a CA of 92°, whereas a lower CA of 82° was observed for MPG0. The CA of other membranes were 61°, 41° and 43° for MPG1, MPG2 and MPG3 respectively (Fig. 7 (A)). The drastic change of CA was observed in the case of pure PSf and MPG2, whereas the smaller CA of MPG2 manifests in its hydrophilic surface. But that noticeable change is not observed while moving from MPG2 to MPG3. Additionally, the enhanced hydrophilicity of the membrane surface promotes its interaction with water molecules as indicated by the highest interfacial surface energy of 89.65 mJ m−2 observed in MPG2. The calculated relative surface energy of MPG2 membrane is obtained as 0.1522 mJ m- 2. Conversely, the CA of MPG1 and MPG3 increased, leading to a lower interfacial surface energy than that of the MPG2 membrane. This phenomenon could be explained by the high concentration of GOQDs, which could accumulate and obstruct membrane pores, leading to a reduction in interface surface energy and a marginal increase in the water CA [22].

To ascertain the electrostatic interactions between the fouling reagents and the membrane surface, zeta potential measurements were conducted. 1 mM KCl solution was used as the electrolyte at pH 7 and room temperature, and the membranes were immersed in the solution for 30 min. The primary membrane displayed a negative ζ potential of −6.5 mV, while the modified membrane showed a surface charge of −8.9 mV due to the formation of a PA layer on the substrate. The zeta potential was further reduced upon the addition of GOQDs into the PA layer. The ζ-potential of MPG1 to MPG2 membranes decreased from −10.6 to −13.7 mV, as the concentration of GOQD increased from 0.2 to 0.4 %. The incorporation of carboxyl groups in GOQD flakes led to a more negatively charged surface. A similar phenomenon was reported in composite membranes containing GO and carbon nanotubes (CNTs). Fig. 7(B) depicts the changes in zeta potential of the composite membranes [26,27]. However, in the case of the MPG3 membrane, the negative surface charge was −12.8 mV, which might be due to GOQD agglomeration when the GOQD concentration was high enough. The zeta potential of all the membranes over a pH range from 2.2 to 8.4 was presented in Fig. S7 of the supplementary material.

In Fig. 7(C), surface roughness characteristics of MPG0, MPG1, MPG2, and MPG3 membranes are evident. It is apparent that as the concentration of GOQDs in the composite membranes increases, the surface morphology undergoes a transformation. MPG0 and MPG1 exhibit relatively smoother surfaces with fewer physical irregularities. However, as the GOQD concentration rises, a transition becomes evident. In the case of MPG2, a notably rougher surface with distinct peaks and valleys is observed, indicating a considerable increase in surface roughness (Ra = 44.1 nm). This enhancement can be attributed to the effective dispersion of GOQDs on the membrane surface. Conversely in MPG3, there is a slight decrease in surface roughness, possibly due to the agglomeration of GOQDs filling irregular surface positions. Additionally, surface deformation is noted. The increased surface roughness in MPG2 enhances the membrane's water permeability and dye rejection capabilities by providing a greater surface area and facilitating improved interactions with solute molecules [28].

The water flux of the membranes are shown in Fig. 8 (A). Out of all the prepared membranes, MPG2 membrane exhibited the highest flux of 187 ± 15 Lm−2h−1. The increased membrane permeability with the modified membrane was attributed to increased hydrophilicity due to PA groups, which were further increased by the addition of GOQDs [25]. However, if the GOQD content was excessively high, the membrane permeability would be compromised, which was found to be the case for membrane MPG3.

The performance of MPG2 membranes for dye rejection was evaluated under 0.2 MPa using anionic and cationic dyes, and some of the physical characteristics of the dyes were given in Table 2 The spatial size of dye molecules is a critical factor influencing membrane separation performance, and it is indeed essential to consider this aspect for a comprehensive evaluation [29–31]. In the case of the MPG2 membrane, which was assessed for dye rejection under 0.2 MPa using anionic and cationic dyes, the observed order of dye rejection (congo red > methyl blue > rhodamine B > crystal violet > methyl orange > methylene blue) is indicative of the membrane's selective permeability. The permeability and rejection performanc of all the membranes were presented as shown in Table 3 .

The rejection behaviour can be attributed to both steric hindrance effects and the Donnan exclusion principle [32]. The presence of carboxyl groups on the edges of GOQDs in the MPG2 membrane results in a negative ζ-potential. As water molecules transport through the porous membrane surface, the membrane exhibits a strong electrostatic attraction to cationic dyes such as crystal violet, methylene blue, and rhodamine B. Conversely, the negatively charged surface of the membrane leads to significant electrostatic repulsion towards anionic dyes like methyl orange, congo red, and methyl blue, resulting in the lower rejection of methylene blue, as depicted in Fig. 8(B).

The high rejection rates for congo red (99.4 %) and methyl blue (98.5 %) can be explained by the molecular characteristics of these dyes. According to Spolnik et al. [33], anionic congo red has a higher tendency to form supramolecular systems in aqueous solutions due to its hydrophobic interactions through its aromatic counterpart. This molecule's propensity for intermolecular hydrogen bonding, pi-pi stacking, and electrostatic attraction, facilitated by numerous –NH2 groups and the azo bond –N=N-, makes congo red more prone to agglomeration in a supramolecular form over the membrane surface. Consequently, congo red is less likely to pass through the membrane pores readily.

In case of salt rejection of the membrane, initially the membrane sheet was compacted for 30 min. Subsequently, the experiment was carried out under a pressure of 0.1 Mpa. The rejection efficiency of the MPG2 membrane for NaCl, MgCl2, Na2SO4, and Mg(NO3)2 was determined by periodically measuring the salt concentrations in both the feed and permeate solutions. The results indicated rejection rates of 96 %, 97.25 %, 98.01 %, and 99.1 % respectively. Due to the uneven pore sizes of MPG0 and MPG1 membranes, their salt rejection performance was very low, but MPG2 showed better salt rejection, as shown in Fig. 9 (A). Moreover, in case of MPG3 membrane, the clogged membrane pores as evident from SEM images shown in Fig. 6 (C1, C2), resulted in a lower rejection.

The development of PA layer on PSf support was assessed by studying the permeability and rejection of the prepared membranes MPG0, MPG1, MPG2 and MPG3. It was shown that, the MPG2 membrane exhibited significantly better rejection of 96 % and 99.1 % for salt containing Cl− and NO3 − ions respectively which is better than the rejection of Cl− and SO4 2− ions in the same membrane. The enhanced rejection may be attributed to the formation of PA bonds within the cavities of β-CD, which occurs as a consequence of IP between specific concentrations of aqueous and organic monomers (3 % of β-CD, 0.3 % of TEPA and 0.4 % of GOQD). Apparently, the use of GOQD gradually enhanced the tunable membrane surface properties with the consecutive development of membrane pores, as shown in SEM image (Fig. 6(B2)). It has also been observed that the improvement in membrane permeability was higher in the case of GOQD incorporated PA layer, which may be due to increased membrane surface roughness. In a nutshell, the performance of MPG2 membrane achieved with regard to permeability and rejection could be attributed to the diverse deposition of thermally stable chemically reactive –CONH– framework.

In order to comprehensively evaluate the enduring stability of all the developed TFN membranes, an extensive long-term operational test was conducted for membranes designated as MPG0, MPG1, MPG2, and MPG3. After a 5 month duration of continuous operation, noteworthy observations emerged as depicted in Fig. S8 in the supplementary material. Notably, the MPG2 membranes exhibited a distinct behaviour in their separation properties, manifesting higher water flux compared to others, albeit with a gradual decline in pure water flux. This phenomenon warrants further investigation into the underlying mechanisms governing this behavior, potentially shedding light on the membrane's structural changes or fouling effects. Conversely, the remaining membranes, i.e., MPG0, MPG1, and MPG3, displayed a robust resilience, showing no significant decline in their separation efficiency over the same period. This resilience may be indicative of the inherent stability of these membranes under the tested conditions, emphasizing the need for a detailed analysis of the interplay between membrane composition and long-term performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Studies on antifouling behaviour of the membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The membrane antifouling behaviour for all the membranes were studied based on FRR, IFR, and RFR. MPG2 membrane showed the highest FRR for (86 %), whereas MPG0, MPG1 and MPG3 membranes had a FRR of 28 %, 65 % and 79 % respectively. The remarkable antifouling property observed in the MPG2 membrane can be attributed to its hydrophilic and negatively charged surface, which facilitates the formation of a water layer between dyes and the surface. This layer enhances the repulsion between BSA and the surface, ultimately leading to improved anti-fouling performance.

The preferential rejection of Cl− in a PA GOQDs based TFN membrane can be attributed to several factors. The selectivity of the membrane is influenced by the charge and size of the ions. Cl− ions are smaller ions, and their higher charge density makes them prone to electrostatic repulsion from the negatively charged PA matrix. This electrostatic repulsion hinders the passage of Cl− ions through the membrane, resulting in higher rejection. The presence of GOQDs in the polyamide matrix can introduce additional selectivity mechanisms. The functional groups on GOQDs, such as oxygen-containing moieties, may interact differently with Cl− and NO3 − ions.

The higher rejection of NO3 − compared to Cl− in the membrane can be attributed to the difference in the hydrated ionic radius and hydration energy between these ions. As a result, NO3 − ions are forming a larger hydrated shell around the ions. The larger hydrated shell of NO3 − makes it more challenging for the ion to pass through the membrane pores, leading to higher rejection. In nut shell, the rejection of anions is influenced by factors such as ionic radius, charge density, and hydration energy, and the specific characteristics of NO3 − contribute to its higher rejection compared to Cl−.

In this work, novel TFN membranes were reported which was fabricated using Quantum Dot-β-Cyclodextrin nanofiller decorated with TEPA on PSf support using QD concentrations in the range of 0–0.6 wt %. A good dispersion of PA-GOQDs over the membrane surface was confirmed through its morphological studies. The concentration of GOQDs used in membrane fabrication had a significant impact on the physical and chemical properties of the TFN membrane, including hydrophilicity, roughness, and ζ-potential. An increase in GOQD concentration led to an increase in these properties, but excessive concentration proved to be detrimental to membrane performance. The best performing membrane MPG2 exhibited a water flux of 187 ± 15 Lm−2h−1 under 0.2 MPa pressure and high dye rejections, with congo red at 99.1 % and methylene blue at 96 %. The membrane also showed 96 % and 99.1 % rejection for salt containing Cl− and NO3 − ions respectively. The PA-GOQD thin film membrane showed enhanced permeability compared with pure PSf membranes. The antifouling property of the MPG2 membrane was improved due to its high hydrophilicity and negative zeta potential. The separation performance of the GOQDbased NF membranes will be further investigated for removal of cationic and anionic dyes from actual dye industry wastewater.

Rajiv Goswami: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Monti Gogoi: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Alimpia Borah: Data curation, Software, Writing - review & editing. Hrishikesh Sarmah: Investigation, Software. Akhil Ranjan Borah: Data curation, Formal analysis, Software. Xianshe Feng: Data curation, Software, Validation. Swapnali Hazarika: Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests:Dr Swapnali Hazarika reports financial support was provided by Council of Scientific & Industrial Research.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors express sincere gratitude to CSIR New Delhi for the research grant, and extend heartfelt appreciation to the Director, CSIR-NEIST for steadfast support in advancing the progress of this research group. Authors acknowledge Analytical Chemistry Group and Sophisticated Analytical Instrument Facility, CSIR NEIST, for some analytical work.
